## Smart contracts are shared state machines executed by the blockchain

So far in `TEM 00UU`, students have mostly seen blockchains as systems for recording
transactions and reaching consensus on ledger history. Smart-contract platforms add a
different idea: the chain can also maintain persistent program state and execute
agreed-upon rules for changing that state.

A smart contract is therefore best introduced not as "magic code on the blockchain,"
but as a public state machine whose transitions are defined by program logic and
validated by the network.

## What makes a smart contract different from an ordinary script

An ordinary local script runs because one machine decides to run it. A smart contract
matters because many nodes agree on the same transition result.

- The contract code is part of the shared system.
- The contract has persistent state, not just one-off computation.
- Users submit transactions that call contract functions.
- Nodes execute or verify those function effects under common protocol rules.

This is why smart contracts are closely tied to platform design, not just programming
syntax.

## Why Ethereum and the EVM matter

Bitcoin includes limited scripting, but Ethereum popularized the idea of a more
general execution environment. The Ethereum Virtual Machine, or EVM, is the abstract
machine that defines how contract bytecode is executed and how state transitions are
accounted for.

Students do not need bytecode details in the first pass. The important ideas are:

- contract logic is executed in a shared deterministic environment,
- storage updates become part of the chain's agreed state,
- and computation is metered rather than free.

## Why gas exists

If arbitrary computation were free, contract platforms would be easy to abuse. Gas is
the mechanism used to meter computation and storage use so execution consumes a priced
resource.

The first teaching intuition is simple:

- every instruction is not equally cheap,
- contract execution has economic cost,
- and gas limits help bound resource usage.

That lets students connect smart-contract design to both engineering constraints and
economic incentives.

## What students should be able to explain

After this concept, students should be able to explain:

- why a smart contract is best understood as a shared state machine,
- how contract calls differ from ordinary local program execution,
- why Ethereum-style systems need a virtual machine model,
- and why gas exists as a metering mechanism.
